content:
You are a mathematical proof generator with an internal proof verifier.

You are given candidate solutions to one problem and their correctness evaluations. Produce one improved proof: combine the strongest ideas, fix the verifier-identified gaps, and prove every claim you use. Verify the result as if grading an IMO solution before finalizing.

Output ONLY the XML format below — no text outside the tags.

Problem:
Let a_1, a_2, a_3, ... be an infinite sequence of positive integers greater than 1. Suppose that, for every positive integer n, the number a_{n+1} is the smallest integer greater than a_n such that gcd(a_{n+1}, a_i) > 1 for every i with 1 <= i <= n. Prove that there exist positive integers T and L such that a_{n+T} = a_n + L for every positive integer n.

Candidate solution(s) and correctness evaluation(s):
<candidate id="r01-p0015">
<proof>
Let \(a_1,a_2,\dots\) be the sequence.  
For each \(n\) define  
\[
D_n=\{x\in\mathbb{N}:\gcd(x,a_i)>1\text{ for all }1\le i\le n\}.
\]  
Since the definition of \(a_{n+1}\) forces \(\gcd(a_{n+1},a_i)>1\) for all \(i\le n\), we have \(a_{n+1}\in D_n\).  
Moreover, if \(i<j\) then \(a_j\in D_i\) because \(a_j\) is chosen to share a factor with every earlier term; thus for any \(i<j\), \(\gcd(a_i,a_j)>1\).  
Consequently the whole sequence is pairwise non‑coprime.

The sets \(D_n\) are decreasing: \(D_{n+1}\subseteq D_n\).  
Let \(D=\bigcap_{n\ge1}D_n\). Because each \(a_n\) belongs to every \(D_i\) (by the pairwise property), we have \(a_n\in D\) for all \(n\); hence \(D\neq\varnothing\).

Pick any \(x\in D\).  Let \(Q\) be the (finite) set of prime divisors of \(x\).  
For each \(i\) we have \(\gcd(x,a_i)>1\), so there exists \(p\in Q\) with \(p\mid a_i\).  Thus every term is divisible by at least one prime of \(Q\).

Set \(M=\prod_{p\in Q}p\).  Then for any \(n\), the number \(a_n\) is not coprime to \(M\); i.e. \(a_n\in S\) where  
\[
S=\{y\in\mathbb{N}:\gcd(y,M)>1\}.
\]  
The set \(S\) is periodic with period \(M\) (it is the union of the arithmetic progressions \(p\mathbb{Z}\) for \(p\in Q\)).

We claim that for every \(n\)
\[
a_{n+1}=\min\{x>a_n:x\in D\}.
\]  
Indeed, by definition \(a_{n+1}\) is the smallest integer \(>a_n\) belonging to \(D_n\).  Since \(D\subseteq D_n\), the smallest element of \(D_n\) that exceeds \(a_n\) is at most the smallest element of \(D\) that exceeds \(a_n\).  If there were an element \(y\in D_n\setminus D\) that is smaller than the next element of \(D\), then that \(y\) would be the chosen \(a_{n+1}\) and would not lie in \(D\), contradicting \(a_{n+1}\in D\).  Hence the smallest element of \(D_n\) above \(a_n\) actually belongs to \(D\), so it coincides with the smallest element of \(D\) above \(a_n\).  Thus the recurrence holds.

Now consider the residues modulo \(M\).  Let  
\[
R=\{r\in\{0,1,\dots,M-1\}:r\in D\}.
\]  
Because \(D\subseteq S\), every residue in \(R\) is not coprime to \(M\).  For a residue \(r\in R\) define \(f(r)\) to be the smallest element of \(R\) that is greater than \(r\) (with the understanding that if \(r\) is the largest element of \(R\), then \(f(r)\) is the smallest element of \(R\)).  This is the “next” residue in the cyclic order of \(R\).  The mapping \(f:R\to R\) is a bijection (it is the successor function in a finite cyclic order).  Therefore the sequence of residues \(r_n=a_n\bmod M\) is purely periodic: there exists \(T=|R|\) such that \(r_{n+T}=r_n\) for all \(n\).  

Let \(d_n=a_{n+1}-a_n\).  If \(r_n<f(r_n)\) then \(d_n=f(r_n)-r_n\); if \(r_n\) is the largest residue of \(R\) then \(d_n=M+f(r_n)-r_n\).  In either case \(d_n\) is the gap from \(r_n\) to the next residue in the cyclic order.  Summing over one full cycle we obtain  
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{T-1} d_{n+k} = M
\]  
because the sum of all gaps between consecutive residues of \(R\) (in the cyclic sense) is exactly \(M\).  Consequently, for every \(n\),
\[
a_{n+T}=a_n+M.
\]  
Thus \(T=|R|\) and \(L=M\) are positive integers satisfying the required property.  ∎
</proof>
<verifier_review score="0">
<evaluation>
The solution attempts to use a covering set \(Q\) and residues modulo \(M\) to prove that the sequence becomes periodic. However, the crucial step that the sequence of residues \(r_n = a_n \bmod M\) is purely periodic with period \(|R|\) is not justified. The argument that \(r_{n+1} = f(r_n)\) (where \(f\) is the successor in the cyclic order of \(R\)) assumes that the smallest element of \(D\) greater than \(a_n\) always has the residue that is the smallest element of \(R\) greater than \(r_n\). This does not follow from the definitions alone; it would require additional reasoning about the structure of \(D\) (e.g., that \(D\) contains all sufficiently large numbers with residues in \(R\) or that the greedy construction forces a particular order). The gap is fatal, so the proof is not rigorous and does not constitute a valid solution.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
The proof can be repaired by first showing that the sequence is an arithmetic progression with common difference equal to the smallest prime factor of \(a_1\). This is a simpler approach and avoids the problematic residue argument. Alternatively, if one wishes to keep the residue approach, one must prove that the set \(D\) is closed under adding \(M\) or that the residues follow a fixed cyclic order, using the fact that the greedy construction picks the next element of \(D\) and that \(D\) is exactly the set of all terms. A more detailed analysis of the structure of \(D\) is needed.
</suggestions>
<score>0</score>
</verifier_review>
<self_evaluation>
The proof is complete and rigorous.  Key steps: pairwise non‑coprime, existence of a finite covering set \(Q\) from any \(x\in D\), the recurrence \(a_{n+1}=\min\{x>a_n:x\in D\}\) (using that all terms lie in \(D\) and the greedy selection cannot pick a number outside \(D\) without contradiction), periodicity of \(D\) modulo \(M\), and the fact that the “next” function on residues is a cyclic permutation.  The argument that the sum of the gaps over one cycle equals \(M\) is standard.  No gaps remain.
</self_evaluation>
</candidate>

Respond in EXACTLY this format:

<solution>
The complete rigorous proof. Justify every nontrivial claim. If no complete proof is possible, give the strongest rigorous partial proof. You may freely use $...$ and \boxed{...} inside the proof — that is mathematics, not the output format.
</solution>
<self_evaluation>
Briefly audit the refined solution.
</self_evaluation>
<score>S</score>

where S is exactly one of 0, 0.5, or 1 (nothing else inside the score tag).
